Investment Strategy

Primarily invests in equity securities of companies focusing on the development, adoption, or utilization of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies identified through our fundamental research as demonstrating promising growth potential. Seeks long-term capital appreciation.

Portfolio Management

Patrick Kelly, CFA

Executive Vice President

Portfolio Manager,

Head of Alger Capital Appreciation and Spectra Strategies

28 Years Investment Experience

Benchmark

S&P 500 Index

Market Environment

U.S. equities ended the fourth quarter on a firm footing, with the S&P 500 Index returning 2.7%, extending the steady grind upward that characterized 2025, after the tariff announcements. Investor confidence was supported by a familiar trio: corporate earnings that came in better than expected, a Federal Reserve (Fed) that pushed further into an easing stance, and a macroeconomic backdrop that — despite mounting cross- currents — remained resilient in the aggregate. Market sentiment also benefited from improving clarity on trade policy, including an extension of the U.S. – China truce and selective tariff relief aimed at easing consumer affordability pressures.

At the same time, the quarter was defined by widening dispersion beneath the index-level surface. The market’s biggest tailwind — continued investment tied to artificial intelligence (AI) — was increasingly accompanied by scrutiny around AI infrastructure bottlenecks, sources of financing, and whether the AI theme could deliver returns commensurate with the capital deployed. Macroeconomic risks also remained in play: labor market softening became harder to ignore, consumers appeared more cautious and bifurcated across income cohorts, and housing stayed under pressure. Complicating the read-through, a prolonged government shutdown reduced economic data visibility, amplifying debate over inflation and employment signals just as global central-bank dynamics leaned less uniformly supportive. Nevertheless, U.S. equities reinforced a constructive baseline for the S&P 500 Index, where Health Care was the top performing sector, while Real Estate was the weakest.

During the quarter, we continued to observe secular themes that we believe are creating attractive investment opportunities—corporations are digitizing their operations, cloud computing is growing and supporting innovation, and AI is at an inflection point, potentially enabling significant increases in productivity. Moreover, U.S. business spending appears to be accelerating, driven by a confluence of factors—most notably burgeoning demand for AI infrastructure and tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill, which allow companies to deduct 100% of eligible equipment and qualifying manufacturing-facility costs in the year placed in service, boosting near-term cash flow and after-tax returns.

Portfolio Update

The Alger AI Enablers & Adopters ETF (ALAI) underperformed the S&P 500 Index during the third quarter of 2025. Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Western Digital Corporation (WDC), and Natera, Inc. (NTRA) were among the top contributors to performance.

Alphabet is the parent of Google and a global leader in digital advertising, cloud computing, and AI-driven consumer services. Shares contributed positively during the quarter as investors increasingly recognized improving execution across the company’s AI strategy, supported by strong engagement trends and rising AI-related workloads. Google Cloud was a key driver, with management highlighting accelerating usage, including increased AI token processing and continued momentum in the Gemini ecosystem. Sentiment was further supported by strengthening cloud demand and new customer wins, as well as reports that Google may broaden access to its TPU capabilities, including potential deployments beyond Google Cloud.

Western Digital is a hard disk drive (HDD) storage company benefiting from rising hyperscaler data-center investment and the continued proliferation of data. The HDD industry is highly consolidated, with only two scaled manufacturers, and Western Digital holds a leading market position. The business has structurally shifted toward cloud customers as consumer exposure has declined, with cloud representing the majority of Western Digital’s revenue. Importantly, industry participants have emphasized capital discipline—prioritizing higher areal density (i.e., more terabytes per drive) rather than adding significant unit capacity—which supports a healthier supply/demand balance and improved profitability. During the quarter, shares contributed positively as the company reported operating results that validated improving fundamentals, including strong revenue and earnings growth driven by higher unit volumes, better-than-guided drive density, favorable pricing, and lower cost per terabyte that helped expand margins.

Natera is a specialty diagnostics laboratory that provides high-value genetic testing across three core franchises: reproductive health (including non-invasive prenatal testing for chromosomal conditions such as trisomy 13, 18, and 21), oncology (led by Signatera, which measures circulating tumor DNA to assess treatment response and detect molecular residual disease and cancer recurrence), and transplant (tests used to monitor organ rejection). These tests are built on the company’s proprietary cell-free DNA liquid biopsy platform, enabling highly sensitive detection from a blood sample. Shares contributed positively after the company reported strong fiscal third-quarter earnings, with management commentary pointing to revenue and margin performance that exceeded expectations on the back of higher test volumes and favorable reimbursement dynamics— particularly within the Signatera franchise—alongside another increase to full-year 2025 revenue and margin guidance.

Nebius Group (NBIS), Sea Limited (SE), and Meta Platforms Inc. (META) were among the top detractors from performance.

Nebius is a provider of AI-focused cloud infrastructure, operating GPU compute capacity across Europe and expanding in the U.S. We view the company as a differentiated “neocloud” beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption, as demand for high-quality GPU compute continues to outstrip available supply of chips, power, and data center capacity. Nebius already operates a first-party data center in Finland and has deployed GPU capacity in Paris, while adding U.S. capacity through a Kansas City, Missouri deployment and a new data center build-out in New Jersey. During the quarter, shares detracted amid a broader selloff in AI infrastructure-related equities as investors grew more concerned about the “circularity” of AI ecosystem financing and the durability of AI infrastructure spending. Despite near-term volatility, Nebius continues to sign large, long-term agreements with tier-one customers, including multi-year AI infrastructure contracts with major cloud service providers, improving revenue visibility as new capacity comes online.

Sea Limited is a global consumer internet company operating three core businesses: Garena, a digital entertainment platform known for developing and publishing online games; Shopee, a leading e-commerce platform in Southeast Asia and Taiwan; and SeaMoney, which offers digital financial services including mobile wallets and payment processing. The company has benefited from strong structural growth in its e-commerce and payments business, along with its gaming business returning to stabilization after years of decline. Shares detracted from performance as investors questioned the pace and durability of Sea’s profitability improvement. Specifically, its largest e-commerce competitor in Latin America lowered free-shipping thresholds, which may increase competitive intensity and pressure margins in the near- term.

Meta is the world’s largest social media company, operating platforms that include Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger. Shares detracted during the quarter as investors focused on management’s guidance for materially higher operating expenses and capital expenditures (CapEx) tied to AI infrastructure investments. Management noted that 2026 CapEx is expected to be “notably” higher than 2025, which pressured forward earnings and free-cash-flow expectations despite strong underlying results. While the company highlighted continued advertising momentum and indicated that revenue growth could remain robust into year-end, investors remained concerned about the magnitude and timing of the AI-related CapEx.

data.title: Li Auto Relegated To Playing Catch-Up
data.description:
For most Chinese NEV makers, 2026 began with a stock rally on potential and actual easing of trade restrictions abroad (plus some policy support). Li Auto (LI)(LAAOF), however, underwhelmed and stood out for the wrong reason, claiming the dubious title of last year's second-most profitable short in Asia Pacific.

With deliveries down year-on-year for the eighth straight month, Wall Street sentiment is cooling. And I can't disagree; Li Auto is looking increasingly unappealing.

Li Auto's dethroning

Li Auto had been the model startup - carving out its own lane in a crowded market by betting on extended range EVs (EREVs) and positioning itself as a premium family brand. It worked like a charm: revenue climbed steadily, and high selling prices supported margins. Its vehicle margin, over 20% in Q3 2024, was among the best in the industry.

In 2024 growth slowed, then came 2025. For the full year, Li Auto delivered 406,343 vehicles, down 19%, which was just about half of the original target. Falling ASP and rising costs have squeezed margins further. Vehicle margin dropped to 15% in Q3 2025.

Share price is down more than 20% over the past year.

I see few reasons for what went wrong.

Strategy aspect

Strategy is a big one. Even as demand kept moving toward pure BEVs and advanced PHEVs, Li Auto stuck to EREVs. Warning signs of early 2024 the L-series began losing steam and delivery guidance was cut - were either missed or ignored. Instead of pivoting then, the company stayed the course: EREVs still accounted for 97.8% of total deliveries in 2025.

Its first BEVs arrived only in September 2025 - almost 18 months after those first cracks. By then, the pivot was already behind schedule, plus production capacity for the new i-series lagged at the very time it was needed most. To make things worse, those BEV models underwhelmed, and Li Mega even had to be recalled, which cut 4.3 percentage points off gross margin in Q3 2025.

Li Auto's interim solution for demand issues has been price cutting and discounting. A more structural fix, 'product realignment,' that is aimed at clearer product identity, faster overseas expansion, and shorter platform iteration cycles, is in the works. But whether that's going to be enough to reignite growth will depend on management - whose credibility has already taken a hit - as well as competition that remains as brutal as ever.

Market factor

To be sure, China's NEV market - which accounts for more than 50% of new car sales - is still vibrant compared to the rest of the world, but the days of hypergrowth seem over. Growth is expected to slow sharply: NEV sales may increase by about 15% in 2026 (down from 28% last year) and vehicle exports by just 4% (versus 21%).

The main reason is changing consumer spending patterns. Chinese buyers have been gravitating toward budget NEV options for some time. Affordability is now under more pressure. Although the trade-in subsidy has been extended, the tax exemption on NEVs has been halved since the beginning of the year, and local incentives are being scaled back due to depleted budgets.

Competition factor

Industry growth may be slowing, but competition certainly isn't. Persistent price wars are destroying profitability - average gross margin sank to 1.8% in December 2025. Then there is consolidation: the top 10 NEV makers now control 95% of the market, up from 60-70% a couple of years ago.

Even in this environment, though, startups like NIO and XPeng, along with newer entrants like Xiaomi and Huawei's HIMA, have delivered strong 2025 performances. That alone must have jolted Li Auto to action since they all target the same higher-end segment.

Its legacy EREVs have been losing ground to PHEVs and BEVs from mature competitors like BYD and Geely, while its own BEVs have struggled to gain traction. The natural result is a loss of market share: Li Auto's share of the NEV market slipped from 4.7% in 2024 to 3.2% in 2025 - to be level with Xiaomi, a company that began deliveries a full five years behind.

Valuation

On sales multiples, especially the EV/sales basis (accounting for Li Auto's cash pile), the stock looks cheap - historically and more affordable than closest peers NIO and XPeng and even heavyweight BYD.

Comparing based on trailing earnings is less useful at the moment given Li Auto's net loss in Q3 2025. A forward P/E of 109.51x reflects analyst expectations for full 2025 earnings to be only slightly positive.

Catalysts

Of course, it's not impossible to make a bullish case for Li Auto - Seeking Alpha writers have been doing exactly that throughout 2025. The main company-specific catalyst would be a meaningful transition to BEVs, ideally as soon as this year. A stronger product mix could reverse the decline in deliveries, but stabilizing gross margins above 20% would require firmer price and cost discipline. Finally, overseas expansion needs to start contributing to revenue in a real way (up from the current 1%).

Earnings event

The upcoming report (expected at the end of February) might reassure, at least on a surface level. After all, Q4 deliveries (109,194 vehicles) hit the upper end of the guidance (100,000-110,000) and looked especially good next to Q3 numbers (93,211).

The fact is, the latest quarterly deliveries are still down year-on-year 17%, and revenue - if it hits the estimated RMB29b - will be more than 30% lower. (Deliveries in January, 27,668 vehicles, were down 7.5%.) Gross margin might recover from the low of last quarter (16.3%) but won't reach Li Auto's historical standard, amid all that discounting and cheaper models.

Analysts expect the company to be back in the green after the Q3 loss, but only slightly. There could be a miss here. The company is clearly busy updating its smart tech (like assisted driving) and, no doubt, working on new BEV releases behind the scenes. That, of course, costs a lot of money.

What the market will be watching intently is how soon this BEV scaling happens. The absence of a big announcement will confirm my thesis, i.e., the end of Li Auto's hyper-growth story. Forward guidance for Q1 2026 will set the stage for the market reaction in the short term.

Conclusion

Yes, 2025 was a soft year for the entire industry, with domestic saturation and incentives overall starting to wind down. But Li Auto bears a disproportionate share of responsibility for what happened.

The carmaker took its success for granted. Its premium positioning has come under real threat from a price war that shows no signs of abating. Its stubborn clinging to longer platform update cycles didn't help. In the world's most competitive EV market, that was a bad call.

2026 is set to be even tougher for the industry. That means Li Auto will have a hard time executing what now needs to be a massive turnaround if it is to stand any chance of restoring growth and margins.

The stock does invite high-risk, high-reward investor types. The rest should probably stay away, at least until they see a credible roadmap - and tangible progress - in executing the new strategy.

data.title: Harbor Active Small Cap ETF Q4 2025 Scorecard: Buys, Sells, And Standouts
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from Harbor Active Small Cap ETF Q4 2025 Commentary.

During the fourth quarter, the Harbor Active Small Cap ETF ("ETF") returned -4.29% ('NAV'), significantly underperforming the Russell 2000® Index, which returned 2.19%. The ETF's horrible stretch of relative performance since March 2025 continued through the fourth quarter.

According to Jefferies, from the April 9 low through year-end, the lowest ROE quintile has beaten the highest ROE quintile by 42%. Given our bias to invest in companies that have sustainable competitive advantages and sustainable cash flows, it is not surprising that the ETF has underperformed by almost 30% since the April 9 low. Although not surprising, it has been painful and depressing.

Contributors and Detractors

During the quarter, the following five stocks detracted from the ETF's relative performance.

Doximity (DOCS) was down 38.7% during the quarter. We had been reducing our position in the stock through September, as the stock had a massive run over the past year and had become fully valued. More recently, despite excellent execution and beating expectations, the stock has come under pressure due to concerns over the emergence of private AI competitor Open Evidence and Open AI's interest in the medical space. We are watching the emerging competitive landscape carefully but remain a fan of the company and management's track record of success.

Although we had cut back our position in Janus International (JBI) aggressively throughout the year, the stock declined 33.5% during the fourth quarter when it announced a shortfall in its recently acquired TMC (metalS) business that serves terminals for less-than-truckload ("LTL") truckers. We believe the shortfall is short term and tied to the slowdown in the LTL trucking market, which we think could soon benefit from an improving freight environment.

Pool Corporation (POOL) was down 25.96% during the quarter despite a slight beat in third-quarter earnings. The pool industry has suffered from a post-COVID hangover, and we have been aggressively buying the stock into weakness. We think it is a fantastic business at a good price where operating results are bottoming up and have the potential to benefit greatly from an improved housing turnover market.

CarMax (KMX) was down over 30% as the used car market continues to be sluggish, and Carvana continues to aggressively expand share against the incumbent players. We sold the entire position despite its extremely depressed price, as its competitive position has deteriorated significantly.

Shift4 Payments (FOUR) was down during the quarter along with many of its peers in the payment processing space. In addition, investors are hesitant about the additional leverage the company took on from its large Global Blue (GB) acquisition.

During the quarter, the following five stocks contributed to the ETF's relative performance:

Liberty Energy (LBRT) was up significantly during the quarter after it emphasized the prospects of its Liberty Power Innovations business in its third-quarter conference call. That business will help provide distributable power solutions to data centers. We continue to view Liberty Energy as a best-in-class operator in the hydraulic fracking space and like the prospects for its newer Liberty Power Innovations business. However, we reduced our position during the quarter, as the stock moved up close to 50% with no significant change in fundamentals or competitive positioning.

Landstar System (LSTR) moved up 17.45%, as some green shoots have emerged in the bottoming of the three-year-old freight recession.

The Middleby Corporation (MIDD) was up 11.59% during the quarter. Much of the gains took place in December, after it announced it would sell a majority stake in its residential kitchen business to private equity. Its residential business is viewed as the worst of its three businesses, and the anticipated sale price looks fair.

Maplebear (CART) (doing business as Instacart) was up 21.94% during the quarter, after being down 19.46% in the previous quarter. The company continues to execute and take share but sold off due to fears of Amazon (AMZN) getting more aggressive in the grocery space.

Winstar Financial (WTFC) was up 5.97% as the Chicago-based bank continued to execute, and regional banks outperformed during the quarter as the yield curve steepened.

Buys and Sells

During the quarter, we purchased the following three stocks.

We added a small position in Figure Technology Solutions (FIGR) after the stock price settled down after its IPO pop. Highly profitable and growing rapidly, the company was founded by the founder and CEO of SOFI. Figure Tech applies the blockchain to its tech-driven platform that is aggressively taking share in the HELOC market. We expect the company to continue to grow in the HELOC market and into adjacent marketplaces. The stock is not cheaply valued on near-term earnings, but we believe it has a strong competitive advantage and has the potential to grow rapidly.

We initiated a small position in the December IPO of Wealthfront Corporation (WLTH). The stock is reasonably priced for a fast-growing, automated wealth management solution platform that caters to younger investors. We are impressed with the company's execution and customer loyalty. The position would be bigger if we weren't so worried about the significant negative impact that lower interest rates could have on its highly successful and profitable cash management product.

After introducing FactSet Research Systems (FDS) to the ETF in September, we continued to add to the position on price weakness. We have followed and admired the company for many years and have never owned the stock because of its high valuation. With strong free-cash-flow characteristics, high recurring revenue, low leverage, and the stock trading 40% off its highs, we believe now is the time to own the stock. Owning a piece of this compounder at a mid-single-digit, free-cash-flow yield is an attractive entry point.

During the quarter, we sold the following three stocks.

We sold our entire CarMax position despite its extremely depressed price, as its competitive position has deteriorated significantly.

We sold our entire Zillow Group (Z) position due to its high valuation and graduation into the mid-cap space.

We sold our entire position in Guardian Pharmacy Services (GRDN) given its high valuation relative to its growth rate. We believe it is a well-run company and would love to get involved in the stock again at much lower prices.

Overweights and Underweights

During the quarter, we increased our exposures in Information Technology, Industrials, and Financials. We decreased our exposure in REITs and Consumer Discretionary and Energy. The risk/reward in many software/business services companies and small-cap industrials looks particularly attractive. In Financials, we added new positions in a few financial technology IPOs and added to some of our financial data providers.

We decreased our exposure in Consumer Discretionary by selling all of CarMax position and trimming Warby Parker (WRBY) after significant price appreciation. Our REIT exposure decreased after trimming our large position size in hotel REIT RLJ Lodging trust (RLJ), which we continue to like and view as trading at a significant discount to private market value. Our Energy sector exposure decreased after trimming our position in Liberty Energy after the stock ran up from its announcements in the data center power supply space.

data.title: Harbor Active Small Cap ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
In our opinion, outside of robust growth in AI, computing power, space, and defense spending, much of the U.S. economy has been bouncing along the bottom since the 2022 peaks. - Byron Place Capital Management

Market in Review

During the fourth quarter of 2025, small caps slightly underperformed large caps with the Russell 2000® Index up 2.19% and the S&P 500 up 2.66%. Within small caps, Health Care was up over 18.55%, led by biotech and pharmaceutical stocks. Biotech and pharmaceuticals represent close to 7% and 2%, respectively, of the Russell 2000® Index. Unfortunately, we have no exposure in either industry. Although there was some exhaustion of the dominant Artificial Intelligence trend during the quarter, non-earners, microcaps, and low return-on-equity ("ROE") stocks continued to outperform. We have not chased the speculative rally and have been purchasing profitable, competitively advantaged businesses that have fallen out of favor and become significantly cheaper. We will continue to remain patient and purchase small-cap companies that have a sustainable competitive advantage and are trading below intrinsic value. We have also added significantly to some of our current ETF stocks that have fallen out of favor, despite no significant changes to their competitive positions. During the quarter, we were particularly active in adding to some of our long-held software and business service stocks that came under pressure.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter, the Harbor Active Small Cap ETF ("ETF") returned -4.29% ('NAV'), significantly underperforming the Russell 2000® Index, which returned 2.19%. The ETF's horrible stretch of relative performance since March 2025 continued through the fourth quarter.

According to Jefferies, from the April 9 low through year-end, the lowest ROE quintile has beaten the highest ROE quintile by 42%. Given our bias to invest in companies that have sustainable competitive advantages and sustainable cash flows, it is not surprising that the ETF has underperformed by almost 30% since the April 9 low. Although not surprising, it has been painful and depressing.

Contributors and Detractors

During the quarter, the following five stocks detracted from the ETF's relative performance.

Doximity (DOCS) was down 38.7% during the quarter. We had been reducing our position in the stock through September, as the stock had a massive run over the past year and had become fully valued. More recently, despite excellent execution and beating expectations, the stock has come under pressure due to concerns over the emergence of private AI competitor Open Evidence and Open AI's interest in the medical space. We are watching the emerging competitive landscape carefully but remain a fan of the company and management's track record of success.

Although we had cut back our position in Janus International (JBI) aggressively throughout the year, the stock declined 33.5% during the fourth quarter when it announced a shortfall in its recently acquired TMC (metalS) business that serves terminals for less-than-truckload ("LTL") truckers. We believe the shortfall is short term and tied to the slowdown in the LTL trucking market, which we think could soon benefit from an improving freight environment.

Pool Corporation (POOL) was down 25.96% during the quarter despite a slight beat in third-quarter earnings. The pool industry has suffered from a post-COVID hangover, and we have been aggressively buying the stock into weakness. We think it is a fantastic business at a good price where operating results are bottoming up and have the potential to benefit greatly from an improved housing turnover market.

CarMax (KMX) was down over 30% as the used car market continues to be sluggish, and Carvana continues to aggressively expand share against the incumbent players. We sold the entire position despite its extremely depressed price, as its competitive position has deteriorated significantly.

Shift4 Payments (FOUR) was down during the quarter along with many of its peers in the payment processing space. In addition, investors are hesitant about the additional leverage the company took on from its large Global Blue (GB) acquisition.

During the quarter, the following five stocks contributed to the ETF's relative performance:

Liberty Energy (LBRT) was up significantly during the quarter after it emphasized the prospects of its Liberty Power Innovations business in its third-quarter conference call. That business will help provide distributable power solutions to data centers. We continue to view Liberty Energy as a best-in-class operator in the hydraulic fracking space and like the prospects for its newer Liberty Power Innovations business. However, we reduced our position during the quarter, as the stock moved up close to 50% with no significant change in fundamentals or competitive positioning.

Landstar System (LSTR) moved up 17.45%, as some green shoots have emerged in the bottoming of the three-year-old freight recession.

The Middleby Corporation (MIDD) was up 11.59% during the quarter. Much of the gains took place in December, after it announced it would sell a majority stake in its residential kitchen business to private equity. Its residential business is viewed as the worst of its three businesses, and the anticipated sale price looks fair.

Maplebear (CART) (doing business as Instacart) was up 21.94% during the quarter, after being down 19.46% in the previous quarter. The company continues to execute and take share but sold off due to fears of Amazon (AMZN) getting more aggressive in the grocery space.

Winstar Financial (WTFC) was up 5.97% as the Chicago-based bank continued to execute, and regional banks outperformed during the quarter as the yield curve steepened.

Buys and Sells

During the quarter, we purchased the following three stocks.

We added a small position in Figure Technology Solutions (FIGR) after the stock price settled down after its IPO pop. Highly profitable and growing rapidly, the company was founded by the founder and CEO of SOFI. Figure Tech applies the blockchain to its tech-driven platform that is aggressively taking share in the HELOC market. We expect the company to continue to grow in the HELOC market and into adjacent marketplaces. The stock is not cheaply valued on near-term earnings, but we believe it has a strong competitive advantage and has the potential to grow rapidly.

We initiated a small position in the December IPO of Wealthfront Corporation (WLTH). The stock is reasonably priced for a fast-growing, automated wealth management solution platform that caters to younger investors. We are impressed with the company's execution and customer loyalty. The position would be bigger if we weren't so worried about the significant negative impact that lower interest rates could have on its highly successful and profitable cash management product.

After introducing FactSet Research Systems (FDS) to the ETF in September, we continued to add to the position on price weakness. We have followed and admired the company for many years and have never owned the stock because of its high valuation. With strong free-cash-flow characteristics, high recurring revenue, low leverage, and the stock trading 40% off its highs, we believe now is the time to own the stock. Owning a piece of this compounder at a mid-single-digit, free-cash-flow yield is an attractive entry point.

During the quarter, we sold the following three stocks.

We sold our entire CarMax position despite its extremely depressed price, as its competitive position has deteriorated significantly.

We sold our entire Zillow Group (Z) position due to its high valuation and graduation into the mid-cap space.

We sold our entire position in Guardian Pharmacy Services (GRDN) given its high valuation relative to its growth rate. We believe it is a well-run company and would love to get involved in the stock again at much lower prices.

Overweights and Underweights

During the quarter, we increased our exposures in Information Technology, Industrials, and Financials. We decreased our exposure in REITs and Consumer Discretionary and Energy. The risk/reward in many software/business services companies and small-cap industrials looks particularly attractive. In Financials, we added new positions in a few financial technology IPOs and added to some of our financial data providers.

We decreased our exposure in Consumer Discretionary by selling all of CarMax position and trimming Warby Parker (WRBY) after significant price appreciation. Our REIT exposure decreased after trimming our large position size in hotel REIT RLJ Lodging trust (RLJ), which we continue to like and view as trading at a significant discount to private market value. Our Energy sector exposure decreased after trimming our position in Liberty Energy after the stock ran up from its announcements in the data center power supply space.

Outlook

We see attractive risk/rewards in early cyclicals in the consumer/industrial space and in the software/business service space. In our opinion, outside of robust growth in AI, computing power, space, and defense spending, much of the U.S. economy has been bouncing along the bottom since the 2022 peaks. Numerous high-quality, small-cap stocks are down 40%–50% from their 2022 peaks, and we believe this is the sector where there will be opportunities, as the broader economy improves and plays catch-up to some of the recent megatrends that have fueled much of the stock market gains over the past three years.

data.title: Alpine Income: 5.3% Dividend Hike And Dual Beats Support Ongoing Rally
data.description:
Alpine Income Property (PINE) is raising its dividend distribution by 5.3% at a time when investors are on the hunt for safe dividend yields in an economic environment that's being defined by volatility and uncertainty. The list of headwinds for REITs is not immaterial. Most notably, recent Fed rate cuts have failed to drag down long-term U.S. Treasury yields, with the 10-year Treasury rate (US10Y) actually higher now than when the Fed first cut at the September 18, 2024, rate-setting meeting. This has kept the yield investors are demanding to hold REITs higher than the pre-pandemic average. The CME FedWatch Tool currently has a more modest 50 basis points of rate cuts through 2026 as its base case, setting up another possibly constrained year for REIT total returns if long-term rates fail to dip in response to these pending cuts. PINE has bucked the broader trend, with REIT last declaring a quarterly cash dividend of $0.30 per share, a 5.3% increase versus its prior distribution, and $1.20 per share annualized for a 5.85% dividend yield. The yield has dipped materially from when PINE was trading at its lows just in October. I last covered PINE in the 2023 rate-hiking cycle.

PINE's dividend raise came on the back of strong fiscal 2025 fourth-quarter earnings. The CTO Realty-managed REIT reported revenue of $16.9 million, up 22.6% over its year-ago comp and a healthy beat by $1.71 million on consensus estimates. Growth was driven by aggressive investment activity, with PINE closing 14 investments with a nominal value of $142 million during the fourth quarter. This boosted PINE's 2025 annual investment activity to a record $278 million. The REIT's investment drive has been a blended mix of property acquisitions and commercial real estate ("CRE") loans, with $102.26 million of fourth-quarter investment activity comprised of CRE credit originated during the quarter. This CRE loan program had $129.8 million in debt outstanding with a weighted average coupon rate of 12.4% and a weighted average remaining term of 1.9 years as of the end of the fourth quarter. There was also a further $45.7 million in unfunded credit at the end of the quarter.

FFO Growth Outlook, Dividend Coverage, and the Preferreds

PINE is guiding for operations ("FFO") to come in at $2.07 to $2.11 on a per-share basis for its full-year fiscal 2026. The REIT generated fourth-quarter FFO of $8.46 million, around $0.54 per share, and a beat by $0.05 per share on consensus estimates. This meant the REIT is covering its recently raised dividend by 180%, with coverage expanding sequentially from the third quarter, when PINE covered the prior distribution by 161% from FFO. Critically, the midpoint of PINE's FFO guidance for 2026 means the REIT is set to grow FFO by roughly 11.17% over FFO per share of $1.88 generated in its full year 2025. A strong double-digit FFO growth outlook, healthy dividend coverage, and an expanding portfolio all put the 8.00% Series A Cumulative Preferreds (PINE.PR.A) into focus.

These pay out a $2 per share annual coupon and, as they're trading hands for $25.29 per share, offer a 7.91% yield on cost. This price also represents a 1.16% premium to their $25 per share redemption price. As a broad rule, I tend to stay away from preferreds trading at a premium to their redemption price, and I won't make an exception for PINE. The common shares are healthy, so going with the preferreds as a strategy to maintain exposure to the REIT's 127 properties and 4.3 million square foot portfolio does not inherently apply here.

Critically, the REIT does not face any debt maturing this year up until 2029, when a $100 million unsecured term loan comes due. The impact of three years of no maturities dramatically enhances the prospect of follow-on dividend hikes, as there are lower refinancing headwinds for PINE to face over the next three years. The REIT also held total debt of $378 million at a weighted average interest rate of 4.50% as of the end of the fourth quarter. This also improves the outlook for the already incredibly safe preferreds.

Conclusion

PINE's dividend hike, depth of dividend coverage, and well-laddered debt maturity wall all materially heighten the case for the ongoing rally of the common shares. The REIT is currently swapping hands at a 9.8x multiple to the midpoint of its FFO guidance range for 2026. This is really healthy, especially against the outlook for further dividend hikes. I am rating PINE as a Buy, with the preferred shares being rated as a Hold.

data.title: Snowflake Stands Apart From SaaSpocalypse Fears (Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As Snowflake (SNOW) prepares to publish its full-year CY25/FY26 ER in just over a week, the company’s shares have been hit by investor confidence.

The end of the month will mark the 2nd anniversary since Snowflake’s current CEO, Sridhar Ramaswamy, took over the reins from ex-CEO Frank Slootman’s abrupt departure as Snowflake reports its ER.

While Snowflake's current management has done well to restore investor confidence and faith in its leadership, the company finds itself once again at a pivotal moment as it wrestles to navigate Snowflake away from the SaaSpocalypse fears plaguing software stocks.

Markets have turned sour on Snowflake’s shares as consensus estimates call for top line growth that is less than management’s projections, clearly showing how bearish the sentiment has changed towards Snowflake.

Amidst all the concern, I believe markets are being dramatic, with Snowflake’s valuation multiples finally coming to appealing levels, leading me to upgrade Snowflake’s shares to a bullish view ahead of Q4 ER.

How Snowflake Stands Out From the Software Pack

When Snowflake issued updates to their long-term growth model last year, I didn't find any catalytic changes to their growth model, especially when the company was then trading at a 128x EV/EBITDA multiple or at ~16x CY25 sales.

Snowflake has always been fighting the image of being a richly valued, fast-growing tech company that uses SaaS metrics like net retention, backlog, etc. to support its valuation. Over the last 2 years, the company has shed the “database company” moniker to be more closely associated with AI, calling itself the ‘AI data cloud platform.’ Snowflake’s Ramaswamy had done well to steer the company in the new age of AI, navigating the company towards a product roadmap that complemented AI’s aggressive data consumption pace.

The pivot to Postgres and the rollout of micro ecosystems like Snowpark and Cortex pushed Snowflake closer towards helping users operate quickly in unstructured data environments and yet become one of the de facto and go-to AI data platforms. That seemed to work very well for Snowflake, which was scoring strong growth in its backlog.

In fact, Snowflake’s Q3 ER showed the first signs of backlog acceleration, where backlog is now growing at a 38% pace to almost $8B. An acceleration in backlog usually leads to an acceleration in Snowflake’s Product revenues as well, which is the bulk of Snowflake's revenues.

So far, that has worked well for Snowflake, with the company’s shares just about breaking even vs. the S&P 500 for the first time since Ramaswamy took over the reins at Snowflake.

But when macro fears hit the tech sector, especially when these fears are as endogenous as AI, investors will question the richest tech companies, and Snowflake’s name will be one of the first to come up. This was the sole sore point I saw with Snowflake’s shares when I published my report in July last year.

At the moment, valuation multiples have crashed. They could probably fall a little bit further, but I believe this is the time to begin buying shares because the markets are acting irrationally with Snowflake, compressing the company’s valuation multiples as a result and making shares look appealing again.

In the upcoming Q4 FY26 ER, Snowflake’s management will face the tough task of addressing the fallout seen in its shares and how the treatment is unjustified because Snowflake’s Intelligence tools complement the narrative of using AI in the workplace.

Before the SaaSpocalypse fears bubbled up, Ramaswamy talked about how Snowflake’s 1.0 value proposition already stood apart from SaaS companies, which “operated basically [as] transactional systems” or a “system of record.” In the age of AI, Snowflake 2.0’s value proposition stands out because:

There's more and more of a realization that there indeed is a closed loop around data. And AI accelerates this because people now understand that if somebody, Snowflake, has a copy of all of the most critical data about a company, it can be the place where decisions can be made about what do you optimize, what do you do next.

In addition, Snowflake has been furiously striking partnerships with its SaaS peers like Salesforce (CRM) and ServiceNow (NOW) to enrich the criticality of the data available on its cloud platform. Intelligence tools like Snowpark and Cortex work as intelligent co-work tools that enhance the value proposition of Snowflake 2.0.

All said and done, what markets will be looking for are two key numbers—acceleration in Snowflake’s backlog as well as in its top-line revenue along with expansion in gross margins.

Per the last known estimates, Snowflake’s management is expecting its product-level gross margins to dip slightly to the 75% level on an adjusted basis. I still believe that 75% margin guidance is conservative, and Snowflake should land its product gross margins closer to the 76% level.

Assuming the margins profile stays stable through FY27, the company begins to look attractive on a forward revenue multiple basis and a forward EBITDA multiple basis.

Snowflake’s Valuation Multiple Back to Looking Appealing

Heading into earnings, Snowflake is trading at attractive multiples vs. FY27/CY26 revenue expectations, which I believe are conservative since they are below management’s mid-twenties growth targets. For now, let us take the FY27 revenue expectations as they are.

Snowflake is expected to deliver 28% revenue growth in FY26 of $4.7B, while analysts expect 24% top-line growth at the midpoint of their estimated range. At their optimistic best last year, markets were comfortable valuing Snowflake at 17x CY25 revenues for 28% revenue growth.

Therefore, given the current 24% top-line growth projections (which are still pessimistic), Snowflake should actually be valued at ~15x forward revenues, which implies ~26-28% upside from current levels. If I factor in management’s mid-twenties growth targets, the warranted revenue multiple increases marginally further.

Risks & Other Factors to Know

However, investors should note from the valuation section above that if pessimism remains, Snowflake's revenue multiple could be compressed further to the ~10x level, implying ~15% further downside.

Reasons for pessimism could be slower than expected FY27 growth numbers or gross margins being revised lower. This is exactly what I will be looking for in the call on Snowflake’s Q4 ER call later this month on Feb 25.

What to look for in the Q4 call:

  • Q4 FY26 expectations: EPS of 27 cents on revenues worth $1.26B (up 27% y/y)
  • Q1 FY27 expectations: EPS of 33 cents on revenues worth $1.31B (up 26% y/y)
  • FY27 expectations: EPS of $1.62 on revenues worth $5.8B (up 24% y/y)

Takeaway

Snowflake’s shares are back to looking attractive before its Q4 earnings as investors cave into the macro SaaSpocalypse fears, pushing Snowflake’s valuation levels to appealing levels.

Investors are undermining just how crucial Snowflake’s value is for the enterprise in the age of AI and are beginning to be irrationally skeptical about Snowflake’s outlook.

I am upgrading my outlook on Snowflake as bullish.

data.title: U.S. Inflation Details Offer Room For Deeper Fed Rate Cuts
data.description:
By James Knightley, Chief International Economist, US

January US consumer price inflation was marginally lower than expected, with headline CPI up 0.2% month-on-month (consensus 0.3%), while core (ex food & energy) came in as predicted at 0.3% month-on-month (0.295% to 3dp). This leaves the year-on-year rates at 2.4% for headline and 2.5% for core, a four-year low, versus 2.7% and 2.6%, respectively in December. Energy prices fell 1.5% MoM, while used vehicle prices dropped 1.8%. Virtually all other components were very well-behaved, with housing costs rising just 0.2%.

The fact that goods prices, excluding food and energy, were unchanged on the month reinforces the narrative that tariffs are not proving inflationary, although there were decent price increases in some of the furniture and appliance categories. That said, import prices aren’t falling and corporates are still paying billions more in tariffs each month versus 12 months ago. Consequently, we are reluctant to rule out some lingering price pressures, particularly with recent trade data suggesting that the pre-tariff import inventory build has been exhausted and trade patterns are normalising.

The one true “hot spot” was airline fares, which rose 6.5% MoM. As such, there is a fair chance we get an even better core PCE deflator print next Friday (probably a 0.2%) given airfares are derived from the PPI numbers rather than the CPI numbers for that inflation metric. Remember that this happens to be the Fed's favoured inflation measure.

Markets have reacted dovishly, with 63bp of Fed cuts now priced versus 57bp ahead of time. Our view remains two rate cuts this year, in June and September, but with the risk that they do more given the cooling jobs story and increasingly benign inflation backdrop.

data.title: Carillon Reams Unconstrained Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Government shutdown creates fog

As the fourth quarter began, the government shutdown disrupted the release of key economic data, creating challenges for both investors and the U.S. Federal Reserve. This lack of visibility contributed to the “fog” referenced by Fed Chair Jerome Powell during his post-meeting press conference in October. Powell likened the Fed’s approach to that of a cautious driver slowing down in foggy conditions, emphasizing the need for prudence amid heightened uncertainty.

Fog lingers even after shutdown ends

Although the shutdown stalemate was resolved, the resulting data gap persisted through the end of the year as collection and reporting processes remained disrupted. The absence of data collection led to the omission of the October Consumer Price Index (CPI) from the series. Key components, such as owners’ equivalent rent, were held flat, artificially dampening inflation readings once November data was published. Labor market data was similarly affected as the lack of October figures eliminated monthly comparisons, and November employment numbers were delayed until after the Federal Reserve’s December meeting. Consequently, policymakers were forced to make decisions without access to a complete data set.

Federal Reserve cuts twice, outlook moderates

Chair Powell initially struck a cautious tone after the October meeting regarding further interest rate cuts. Labor data was notably uncertain as the last reliable report from September indicated that overall payrolls exceeded expectations, while private payrolls declined sharply and the unemployment rate edged up to 4.4%. His view was later countered when several influential Fed governors adopted a more dovish stance in November, leading to a 25-basis point (BP) rate cut at the December meeting. A modest uptick in equity market volatility in mid-November, driven by investor concerns over the scale of ongoing artificial intelligence (AI)-related capital expenditures, reinforced this dovish sentiment. Looking ahead, the pace of rate cuts is expected to moderate, with Fed voter opinion less uniform (more dissents during votes and variance in dot plot analysis) and markets pricing in only two cuts for calendar year 2026.

Judicial review of tariffs not yet resolved

In early November, investor attention turned to oral arguments before the U.S. Supreme Court regarding whether the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) grants the president the authority to impose tariffs without congressional approval. While no ruling was issued by quarter-end, market participants closely analyzed the arguments for indications of the court’s leanings. Consensus expectations point toward some curtailment of executive authority; however, uncertainty will persist until a final decision is rendered.

Spread sectors were mostly positive during the fourth quarter

Excess returns from mortgage-backed securities (MBS) led the way in the fourth quarter, finishing the year strong after lagging in the first half. Commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) and asset-backed securities (ABS) both had positive excess returns. In credit, high yield finished in positive territory, but investment grade (IG) credit was modestly negative after BBB-rated securities underperformed. The yield curve steepened again in the fourth quarter as the two Fed rate cuts pushed the front end of the curve lower, while the long end rose, indicating some wariness of higher inflation over time. The curve is now inverted out to the 1-year point but normalizes after.

Portfolio Review

Rate volatility continued to decline in the fourth quarter, reaching its lowest levels since 2021 near year-end, despite lingering uncertainty around data related to the government shutdown. Three consecutive 25-bp rate cuts by the Fed at the end of 2025 pushed short-term yields lower, bringing down the front end of the curve. Notably, the yield curve steepened as long-term rates remained elevated, normalizing the term structure. Despite the Fed's dovish stance late in the year, longer-term inflation expectations remained largely in check. The Fund's absolute duration positioning benefited performance during the quarter, as it had throughout 2025.

Treasury exposure was the largest positive contributor during the quarter as carry remained positive. Allocations to MBS, both agency and non-agency, were also large contributors as the asset class saw positive carry and continued spread compression throughout the quarter. The focus on the belly of the coupon curve saw positive selection effect as this portion of the curve continued its strong run of performance, generating the highest levels of excess return in the asset class. The allocation to both investment grade and high yield corporate securities saw positive performance as spreads continued tightened throughout the quarter despite being near historical levels, ending the year at close to where it began 2025 despite second-quarter volatility. ABS exposure saw continued positive performance as the team remains focused on high-quality, income-producing securities in the short duration portion of the curve. Structured macro trades provided a tailwind during the quarter. Limited exposure to CMBS was a marginal benefit during the quarter. Emerging Markets and foreign exchange were the only absolute detractors during the quarter but were positive contributors for the year.

The allocation to agency MBS grew during the quarter as spreads widened following the significant tightening in September.

Non-agency mortgages also increased during the quarter, with the team noting a widening gap in the spread between non-agency and agency securities. The allocation to IG corporates decreased slightly, as did the high yield allocation. Spreads across the corporate spectrum declined during the quarter and the team took profits and allocated elsewhere as opportunities arose. ABS exposure declined quarter over quarter following the large increase noted in the third quarter but remained an area of attractive opportunity. The weight in CMBS remained flat and continued to reside at a low absolute exposure as this asset class continues to require selectivity with limited issuance that meets the high bar for consideration. The exposure to non-U.S. dollar bonds decreased and the allocation to non-U.S. dollar currencies remained largely flat, ending the quarter with a small net non-U.S. exposure. The weight in U.S. Treasuries decreased during the quarter.

Portfolio duration marginally increased during the quarter as the yield curve continued to steepen. Duration remained slightly above neutral range, reflecting real rates that are attractive on a long-term basis but acknowledging the possible contribution to volatility of rates. Yield curve exposure was distributed across the curve with a focus on the 5- to 7-year and 7- to 10-year segments of the curve.

Outlook

Supreme Court tariff ruling likely to add uncertainty

Following the Supreme Court's oral arguments, consensus expectations suggested the justices were inclined to limit the Trump administration's authority to impose tariffs, affirming the appellate court's ruling that deemed the action unlawful. Beyond the overall judgment, the details of the decision are likely to drive near-term market reactions. For instance, if the administration is required to refund previously collected tariffs or faces significant restrictions on imposing new ones, this could stimulate additional economic activity. However, the fiscal implications would be considerable, as tariff revenues are already allocated to fund recently enacted spending. Moreover, rebating prior tariffs could present substantial administrative challenges. As a result, this issue remains a key source of uncertainty as the new year begins.

Will the AI hyperscaler boom continue?

Risk markets took a bit of a pause in November as concerns mounted over the substantial debt financing required to build large-scale data centers to support growing AI storage demands. While AI has the potential to represent a transformative paradigm shift, the sustainability of current growth levels will only be confirmed in hindsight. In the near term, this investment surge may help fuel broader economic expansion. However, if the spending cycle falters, it could create significant headwinds for numerous ancillary industries.

The Fed may slow the pace of rate cuts

The Fed cut rates in each of its last three meetings of 2025. As 2026 begins, that pace of cuts is expected to slow, with the market anticipating only two cuts for all of 2026. The dot plots indicate the Fed has become much more divided. While the current committee is clearly divided, in a few months President Trump is expected to nominate a new chair who may be more sympathetic to the lower rates desired by the administration. Additional clarity from data releases following the government shutdown should provide a better near-term picture of the path of inflation and the health of the labor market, but as always, this provides only a dim view into the future.

'Everything is going to be OK' is still a crowded trade

Current market valuations provide limited compensation for what we view as significant risks embedded in an overly optimistic narrative. Investor sentiment remains elevated despite a growing list of concerns. In this environment, a prudent approach involves minimizing the cost of maintaining a defensive posture while patiently awaiting an inevitable correction, rather than aggressively pursuing higher-risk assets that offer only modest upside if the thesis proves correct.

Definitions

Allocation effect – A measure of the return generated by allocating portfolio weights to particular sectors, industries, segments, or geographies.

Artificial intelligence (AI) – A technology that enables computers and machines to simulate human learning, comprehension, problem solving, decision making, creativity, and autonomy.

Basis points (BPs) – Measurements used in discussions of interest rates and finance. One basis point equals 1/100th of 1%, or 0.01%.

BBB-rated securities – Securities at the lower end of the investment grade rating spectrum.

Capital expenditures / capex – Monies used by a company to buy, improve, or maintain physical assets such as real estate, facilities, technology, or equipment, and may include new projects or investments.

Carry – The net return or cost of holding a bond over a specified period of time, based on the difference in the income it generates and the cost of financing the holding.

Consensus estimates – Forecasts of a public company's projected earnings, the results of a particular industry, sector, geography, asset class, or other category, or the expected findings of a macroeconomic report based on the combined estimates of analysts and other market observers who track the data in question.

Consumer Price Index (CPI) – Measures the change in prices paid by consumers for goods and services. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics bases the index on prices of food, clothing, shelter, transportation, doctors' and dentists' services, drugs, and other goods and services that people buy for day-to-day living. Prices are collected each month in 75 urban areas across the country from about 6,000 households and 22,000 retailers.

Correction – A decline in the market price of a security or index of more than 10% from its recent highs but not more than 20%.

Coupon curve – A line that represents the relationship between coupon rates of similar bonds – for example, those with issuers or credit ratings in common – and their time to maturity.

Credit spread – The difference in yield between a U.S. Treasury bond and another debt security with the same maturity but different credit quality. Also referred to as “bond spreads” or “default spreads,” credit spreads are measured in basis points, with a 1% difference in yield equaling a spread of 100 basis points. Credit spreads reflect the debt security's risk compared with that of Treasury bond – which is considered to be risk-free – and they are considered to be “tighter” when the spread is smaller or “looser” when the spread is larger.

Credit spread compression – A narrowing of credit spreads, often happening when strong demand brings down the yield on a higher-yielding bond.

Credit spread tightening / widening – The contraction (tightening) or expansion (widening) of credit spreads in response to changes in economic conditions that cause credit risk to decline (causing tightening) or increase (causing widening).

Defensive – Investment strategies characterized by rebalancing the investment portfolio regularly to maintain an intended asset allocation. They typically entail investing in high-quality, short-maturity bonds and blue-chip stocks, diversifying across sectors and countries, and holding cash and cash equivalents in down markets.

Dot plot – A U.S. Federal Reserve chart summarizing the Federal Open Market Committee's (FOMC) outlook for the federal funds rate. Each dot represents the interest rate forecasted by one of the 12 members of the committee.

Dovish, hawkish and centrist – Characterizations of the monetary policy preferences of central bankers and others. Doves tend to support maintaining lower interest rates, often in support of stimulating job growth and the economy more generally. Hawks prioritize controlling inflation and may favor raising interest rates to reduce it or keep it in check. Centrists tend to occupy the middle of the continuum between tight (hawkish) and loose (dovish) monetary policy.

Duration – A measure that incorporates a bond's yield, coupon, final maturity and call features into one number, expressed in years, that indicates how price-sensitive a bond or portfolio is to changes in interest rates. Bonds with higher durations carry more risk and have higher price volatility than bonds with lower durations.

Fiscal policy – The tax collection and spending a government uses to influence its country's economy.

Foreign exchange, forex, FX – The trading of one currency for another.

Headwind – An event or market force that hinders the prospects for performance in an individual investment or group of investments.

High yield – Bonds with credit ratings below BBB- from Standard & Poor's or below Baa3 from Moody's.

Hyperscalers – The largest cloud computing providers that can provide massive amounts of computing resources and storage at enterprise scale.

International Emergency Economic Powers Act / IEEPA – A U.S. law that provides the president broad authority to regulate a variety of economic transactions following a declaration of national emergency. In 2025, the act was used to impose or raise U.S. tariffs on imports from many countries.

Investment grade – Fixed-income securities rated BBB or better by Standard & Poor's or Baa or better by Moody's.

Monetary policy – The decisions made by central banks to raise or lower benchmark interest rates or otherwise tighten or loosen credit to influence an economy's growth, inflation, or employment levels.

Overweight – describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Owners' equivalent of rent of primary residences / OER – A component of the Consumer Price Index that helps measure changes in the cost of shelter in the United States. It is based on the answers from consumers who own their homes to the question: "If someone were to rent your home today, how much do you think it would rent for monthly, unfurnished and without utilities?"

Payroll report, officially known as the Employment Situation Summary – A monthly U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) report tracking nonfarm payroll employment and the national unemployment rate, with data on changes in average hourly earnings, and job trends in public and private sectors of employment. The report is based on surveys of households and employers.

Positioning – Assessments of whether professional investors are, on the whole, bullish or bearish on a particular security, industry, sector, market capitalization or other area of the market, as reflected by the extent to which they are invested in the area of the market in question.

Real interest rate – is an interest rate that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation. Once adjusted, it reflects the real cost of funds to a borrower and the real yield to a lender or to an investor.

Securitized products – Products, such as asset-backed securities (ABS) and mortgage-backed securities (MBS), that are created by pooling loans from a variety of sources and issuing bonds that are backed by these loans. Creditworthiness stems from the credit quality of the underlying loans, as opposed to corporate bonds in which creditworthiness is derived from the earning power of the issuing company.

Spread sector – Nongovernmental fixed-income securities, such as corporate bonds or mortgage bonds, whether investment grade or high yield, that provide an additional yield, or "spread," over the yield of a risk-free government bond.

Tailwind – is a term used to describe events or market forces that exert a positive influence on an investment's performance.

Underweight – A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is less than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Valuation – The current or projected worth of an investment. Absolute valuations reflect the absolute value of an investment considered singly, and relative valuations consider the value of an investment against comparable investments.

Visibility – The degree to which market observers and participants can reliably estimate the direction and extent of future performance or other trends in securities, sectors, asset classes, markets, or economies.

Yield curve – A line that plots yields (interest rates) of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. The slope of the yield curve gives an idea of future interest rate changes and economic activity. A steepening yield curve results from a widening in the difference between short- and long-term interest rates. Investors and market analysts watch certain yield curves for signs of inversion, when yields for longer-term debt instruments fall below yields on short-term debt with the same credit quality. The front or short end of the yield curve refers to groups of bonds with the shortest maturity dates. The belly of the curve refers to bonds that mature generally in 2 to 10 years. The long end of the curve refers to bonds with maturities of 10 years or more.

Benchmark Index

The ICE BofA US 3-Month Treasury Index measures the performance of a single issue of outstanding treasury bill which matures closest to, but not beyond, three months from the rebalancing date. The issue is purchased at the beginning of the month and held for a full month; at the end of the month that issue is sold and rolled into a newly selected issue.
