data.title: Waste Management: A Top Defensive Stock For A Bumpy 2026
data.description:
In late November 2025, I reiterated my buy rating on Waste Management, Inc. (WM) and expressed my confidence regarding the company's fundamentals and outlook. So far, that update has aged relatively well, with the stock up around 8% since then. The company reported their latest earnings quite recently but missed on both the top and bottom lines. That might be a surprise for a company that has demonstrated stability and resilience for decades. Today, I will analyze their results and see if there is anything to worry about for shareholders.

Below, it is shown that Waste Management's top line growth rate has suffered, likely as a result of an unfavorable environment. However, Q4 was still a resilient quarter as margin improvements came to the rescue. Furthermore, cash flows looked solid, and heading into 2026, capital returns are set to increase. As for their guidance, the coming fiscal year is expected to look quite a bit like 2025 Q4, but that's not necessarily a bad thing. With the valuation being quite distant below the mid 2025 multiyear high, I believe long term investors are presented with an opportunity to buy or add to positions. Therefore, I'm reiterating my buy rating on Waste Management.

Revenue Growth Dives

Let's get started with a look at their top line results. For 2025 Q4, Waste Management posted revenues of $6.313 billion. While a growth rate of 7% isn't bad, you can clearly see above that there have now been two straight quarters of deceleration. Furthermore, the slowdown from Q3's 14.9% increase has been rather sharp. As stated above, the company also missed expectations, as they underperformed estimates by a considerable margin of $73.72 million. We'll have to look for answers in their guidance later in this article, but judging from these top line results, it does seem that this macro environment is not in their favor. YoY, Collection and Disposal core price dropped from 6.7% to 6.3% as a signal of headwinds. Overall, the company seems to be losing some momentum in its business.

While Waste Management doesn't like to list the growth seen in their Healthcare Solutions since the segment is still a relatively small contributor to overall revenues, there is no question that their push in this direction is starting to pay off. As you can see in the table above, Healthcare Solutions helped to accelerate the top line growth rate for the company significantly both for Q4 and the full year, with their Legacy Business showing some sluggishness. To be specific, in Q4, their Healthcare Solutions saw a 53% increase in revenues YoY. This shows that management's strategic decision to expand into the healthcare waste sector is justified.

Margins Improve & EPS Growth Picks Up

While the deceleration for revenue growth is definitely undesirable, Waste Management's EBITDA margin strength helped to offset some of that weakness. Adjusted EBITDA is one of the company's preferred measures of profitability, and I agree that this metric does a good job at summing up the company's overall business efficiency.

For the company as a whole, adjusted EBITDA margin was 31.3%, and that represents a sizeable expansion of 240 basis points YoY. This is no small expansion, and the fact that both their Legacy Business and their Healthcare Solutions saw YoY improvement makes it even more of a sign that their business is experiencing broad efficiency gains.

For their Legacy Business segment, factors that contributed to margin expansion included disciplined cost initiatives, while for their Healthcare Solutions, the integration of the segment into their overall business has led to "improved processes and synergy capture." Therefore, as a whole, there are great signs for Waste Management's profitability.

As a reflection of the margin strength, Waste Management saw solid levels of bottom line growth. For Q4, their adjusted EPS of $1.93 represented YoY growth of 14%. This is quite a nice acceleration from just a 1% increase seen back in Q3. Even though the company still missed expectations by $0.02, I would say the fact that they were able to accelerate the bottom line while top line growth slowed is an impressive feat of business efficiency. With the macro environment not being the most favorable, it seems Waste Management has put a significant focus on improving the internals of the business. Investors should be encouraged by this, as it means they are positioning the business well for the long term.

Cash Flows & Returns

Overall, Waste Management's cash flows are in good shape, and that reflects a healthy business. In Q4, they had operating cash flows worth $1.698 billion, and you can see above that this represents a respectable growth rate of 12%. This shows that cash generation is strong. There was a large difference between operating cash flow growth and FCF growth, but that was because CapEx moderated significantly. Nonetheless, shareholders seeing FCF growth at multiyear highs should be happy.

While the company didn't do any stock buybacks in 2025 and their dividend yield isn't the highest, there are some signs that the company is willing to share these strong cash flows with shareholders. In mid-December, it was announced that the company would increase the 2026 dividend by 14.5%, and so investors looking for income may be attracted by that. Furthermore, a $3 billion share repurchase authorization was approved, and so moving forward, there will likely be considerably more capital returns than in 2025. Therefore, the strong cash flows seem to be making a real difference for shareholders.

Full Year Outlook

Let's now take a look at the numbers Waste Management expects for the full year 2026. For revenues, they see a range of $26.425 billion to $26.625 billion. This is midpoint growth of 5.2%. This is quite a drastic drop from 14.2% in 2025, and so it seems that weak business momentum will continue due to an unfavorable environment.

For adjusted EBITDA margin, they are projecting 30.8% to 31.0%. The midpoint presents a 30 basis point expansion. For 2025 as a whole, there was a 40 basis expansion, but nonetheless it seems that margin strength will carry on into 2026. The company is, therefore, once again counting on business efficiency improvements to power profitability.

Lastly, for FCF, they believe the figure should be in the range of $3.75 billion and $3.85 billion. A midpoint growth rate of 29.4% for 2026 would outpace the 2025 increase of 26.8%, and so shareholders need not worry about the company's capital returns. As a whole, I would say 2026 is set to look quite a bit like 2025's Q4. Not great revenue results, but margins and cash flows will likely step in to save the day.

Valuation Analysis

Waste Management's forward P/E ratio has bounced back from January lows, but at a current reading of 28.55, it remains well off the multiyear high seen near mid-2025. I believe the stock remains attractive based on where the valuation is.

In today's analysis, it was shown that the company is not operating in a particularly favorable environment. The slowdown in revenue growth shows that clearly. However, the company has managed to make significant improvements internally to boost profitability. As discussed earlier, FCFs and capital returns are looking favorable for shareholders, and full year 2026 guidance shows that this will continue.

While I believe that the forward P/E ratio is justifiably below its 2025 high, I also find the valuation a bit low relative to what we have seen in recent years, as the company is being increasingly well run by its management team. The fundamentals are solid, and what the company really needs is just a change in fortunes with the macro environment. Therefore, long term investors should take advantage of the current valuation.

Concluding Remarks

Given how 2025 ended and how 2026 has started for the market, defense may be the best offense right now for investors. I believe at current levels, a great choice could be Waste Management stock. The company has demonstrated resilience and stability time and time again, and with management focused on the internals of the business, this resilience will only get stronger in my view. Furthermore, the growing dividend and new buyback authorization are great rewards to help shareholders sit through the company's own bout of top line sluggishness, and so I find Waste Management to be quite attractive at the current valuation. As a result, I've decided to maintain my buy rating on the stock.

data.title: XP Inc.: Shifting Its Advisory Strategy, And Trades At Attractive Earnings Multiple
data.description:
XP Inc. (XP) released 4Q25 results, showing a general continuation of the trends we have been commenting on in previous articles: relative stagnation in new clients and net new money, but good performance in asset growth thanks to high-interest rates.

A significant move regarding the qualitative concerns I raised in previous articles is the recognition of service and reputational challenges with the advisor model (which I have long considered problematic). The company shared a new strategy that will govern the advisors more strictly and offer clients the possibility of using a fee-based model.

Although the price has moved up around 12% since I last wrote about XP, I continue to believe the name is attractively priced. Even assuming a depreciation of the BRL, the name trades at 12x TTM earnings, with the possibility to grow under the current model, but also to have a cyclical shift towards the much more profitable equity-heavy allocation regime, if rates go down in Brazil. I maintain a Buy rating.

4Q25 results

The company's results were not particularly surprising for anyone following XP throughout the past two or three years.

We maintain a slow gain of clients (2% YoY), with a stagnant but still interesting R$20 billion addition of net new retail client money (R$32 billion when considering corporate clients). This represents organic, ex-financial growth in client assets (of ~R$1.5 trillion) of around 8%.

In addition to the net new client money, the company's client assets (the ultimate drivers of profitability) are impacted by the high-interest rates in the country (15%). If I have a simple interest rate product with XP, even if I don't invest any new money, as long as I don't redeem money, the assets will grow at 15%. The combination of net new money and the appreciation under several categories of assets (equities or derivatives do not benefit from this) led to total client assets + AUM + AUA to grow 22% YoY. Client assets, a more restrictive consideration, grew 16%.

Net revenues, the next line in the results, grew 10%. We can notice here some challenges in terms of margins or take rate, as seen below.

Margins were broadly stable, with EBT also up 10% for the year. For the quarter, EBT was up 20%, with margins up 250, but this was driven by a particularly good quarter in corporate & issuer (investment banking) services, which is a little more lumpy.

SG&A was up 10% for the quarter and 8% for the year, driven partly by people (headcount up 9%, people expenses up 5%), and non-people (mainly technology investments), up 13%. XP's business should show better operational leverage, but the company has said that it is investing in its service levels (more on this below).

In terms of sources and allocations, things did not change much. Equities presented a positive YoY quarter (+3%), something that had not happened in a long time, but were down for the year (-5%). Fixed income was up 12% (again, in line with national interest rates), and other services gained share (retirement plans, credit cards, insurance), in the aggregate up 16% for the year. This trend has already been analyzed, as XP moves from a broker to a more 'bank'-like 'offering to its clients, with a focus on financial services more than loans.

Also, as commented in previous quarters, the investment banking side of the company (Corporate & Issuer Services) is also gaining share, up 20% for the full year (and 50% for the year in the quarter, as mentioned). The big driver of this movement is XP's capacity to collocate debt among retail clients.

Changes in the advisory model

Since I started covering XP, I have been a critic of the company's advisory model, which was mostly based on transaction fees. This obviously creates a conflict of interest between the advisor and the client, at least in the short term. The potential risk from this was reputational damage to the company if advisors recommended bad products.

Since 2Q25, XP has been revising its advisory model, and during this quarter's call, the company provided more comments about strengthening governance (that is, controlling more), for example, via technology and KPI monitoring of client portfolios, while offering clients the possibility to choose a fee-based model instead of a commission/transaction-based model (25%+ of AUA is already under this modality). The company also provided data on advised portfolio performance (citing, for example, that 90% of portfolios did better than 90% of the national interest rate).

This all indicates an understanding of the risk the transaction-advisory model implies, and I believe it is a healthy development. Aligned advisor-client interest ends up driving higher balances and higher revenues down the road, while protecting the brand.

Valuation remains attractive

XP generated EPS of R$9.8 for the year and R$2.5 in the quarter. Both come to about $1.85/1.9 at the current exchange rate (R$5.2). Compared to that, a stock price of $20 represents a P/E TTM of about 10.5x.

This does not seem like a lot for a company growing the top line and earnings by about 10% YoY. Furthermore, the company is well-positioned for two potential Brazilian macro scenarios.

If rates remain high, then the company will continue to grow on 'autopilot' simply because its assets under custody grow. If rates decrease, the company can benefit from higher debt issuance activity (companies will try to refinance at lower rates) and also from clients shifting towards higher revenue-generating equity products. This will partly be offset by a lower float gain as rates decrease.

A negative scenario would be rates decreasing while the stock market underperforms, which could happen either because there is a recession in Brazil or because of political volatility (the country will have elections at the end of 2026).

Finally, an additional risk is the depreciation of the BRL (the majority, if not all, of the company's earnings are denominated in BRL). If the BRL depreciated towards 6.2 per USD (the lowest point reached in 2024), then at current earnings capacity, the company would generate $1.6 per share in net income, or a P/E of 12.5x, not particularly high either.

I believe XP continues to present an attractive valuation, and therefore maintain a Buy rating.
